sentence: Subjective:
HPI: Ashley, a 14-year-old female, was previously healthy, now here for evaluation of abnormal renal ultrasound with an atrophic right kidney.
She was found to have a small kidney on one side.
She was previously treated for a stomach virus, but her symptoms worsened, and an ultrasound revealed the atrophic right kidney.
She has had multiple hospitalizations for fevers and symptoms, which stopped after a few months.
She has been experiencing stomach pain, burning, and difficulty starting to urinate, especially after drinking certain drinks.
She takes 40 milligrams of Pepcid daily and claritin for allergies.
Family history includes renal disease in her father, aunt, and cousins, and breast cancer in her mother.
Review of systems: She complains of stomach pain, burning, and difficulty starting to urinate, especially after drinking certain drinks.
Current medications: Pepcid 40 milligrams daily, claritin for allergies.
Objective:
Vital signs: Not mentioned.
Physical exam: The patient is a well-appearing female, alert and oriented, with no acute distress.
Her physical exam is otherwise within normal limits.
Test Results: Not mentioned.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has an atrophic right kidney and a history of recurrent fevers and symptoms suggestive of kidney problems, which may be related to a possible severe infection at a young age.
Problem list: Atrophic right kidney; suspected kidney disease; risk of developing high blood pressure and proteinuria.
Plan:
I will recommend an annual ultrasound to monitor the size of her kidneys and their overall function, complete a urinalysis to ensure no protein and blood in her urine, and do a blood pressure check.
I will also recommend avoiding non-steroidal anti-inflammatory medicines like ibuprofen and opting for tylenol instead.
I will instruct her to stay well-hydrated by drinking at least two liters of water per day, maintain a healthy diet, and continue her active lifestyle.
I will recommend that she meet with her primary care physician or an adult nephrologist for follow-up care closer to her home.
Follow-up: She can meet with her primary care physician or an adult nephrologist for follow-up care closer to her home.
